entities.description: The immediate source edition and article for CME00067. MacCracken introduces and transcribes the anonymous poem from Trinity R.3.21, rejects Lydgate's authorship, and supplies a local note about a disordered stanza and a conjecturally missing rhyme word.
entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken's Magnificencia Ecclesie

entities.description: A fifteenth-century composite paper manuscript of miscellaneous Latin and Middle English poems. Magnificencia Ecclesie begins at f. 285r and ends at f. 287r; the next cataloged works begin on f. 287v. The inherited contents heading attributes the poem to Lydgate, but that attribution is disputed and the work is cataloged as anonymous.

holding.notes: Given by George Wilmer; formerly owned by John Stow. The official catalog separately records the poem as item 60 at f. 285.

reviewNotes: Whole-object caching is used throughout: the Trinity bundle contains all 686 canvases exposed by its manifest, the PMLA PDF contains all 952 provider pages including bindings, issue wrappers, advertisements, and blanks, and the Brown PDF contains the complete 498-page provider object.

The work portion is a mapping inside each complete object, never a cropped substitute: the manuscript poem is ff. 285r-287r, Trinity canvases 573-577, while the MacCracken article is printed pp. 687-698 and PDF pp. 743-754 in the complete PMLA scan.

Complete means all provider-exposed canvases or pages. Trinity exposes covers, boards, leaves, flyleaves, and envelope matter but no distinct spine, head, tail, or fore-edge canvases, so its IIIF object is not a complete physical-surface study.
The medieval poem, MacCracken's editorial article, the University of Michigan encoding, provider image derivatives, and access or publication policies are modeled as distinct rights layers.

MacCracken's Westminster localization and rejection of Lydgate are edition-level arguments; the manifest does not conflate manuscript production, later codex assembly, ownership, and medieval work composition.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is copied from University of Michigan CME item CME00067 and encodes the full textual matter of Henry Noble MacCracken's twelve-page 1909 PMLA article, including his introduction, a seventeen-line Longfellow quotation, the complete 312-line Middle English poem, notes, explicit, and signature. MacCracken transcribed the poem from the sole witness listed in the consulted register, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21, ff. 285r-287r. The complete 686-canvas Trinity IIIF provider object is cached at 1600-pixel width, with the poem mapped only to canvases 573-577; f. 287v begins adjacent works. A complete 952-page Harvard/Google scan of the 1909 PMLA volume and complete PDF, OCR, structured OCR, and scan-data derivatives are also cached, as is the complete 1920 Brown register volume containing entry 459.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet
catalogSummary.date: Circa 1475; attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in 1909
catalogSummary.title: Magnificencia Ecclesie

catalogingSubject.label: Magnificencia Ecclesie
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English verse tract conventionally called Magnificencia Ecclesie: thirty-nine eight-line stanzas on the spiritual meanings of the church building, translated from an unidentified Latin source. The record keeps the abstract medieval work distinct from its Latin source, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21, MacCracken's 1909 article and edition, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and modern reproductions. MacCracken's prose introduction and seventeen-line Longfellow quotation are edition paratext, not parts of the medieval poem.

assertion.notes: Summit labels the anonymous poem circa 1475; MacCracken gives the latter half of the fifteenth century. This dates the Middle English work, not every booklet or the present composite codex.

dateStatements.display: Circa 1475; more broadly the latter half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The official catalog dates the physical manuscript only to the fifteenth century; narrower dating belongs to attributed scholarship and remains separate from work composition.

dateStatements.display: Attested in fifteenth-century Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21

assertion.notes: Brown cites MacCracken as the poem's edition; no earlier printing was found in the consulted sources, but a complete publication-history audit remains possible.

dateStatements.display: First known publication in MacCracken's PMLA article, December 1909

extent.description: One complete represented medieval poem: thirty-nine eight-line stanzas and 312 direct verse-line elements. The separate introduction contributes seventeen modern English quotation lines, yielding the XML-wide total of 329 L elements. Eleven numbered printed-page breaks mark pp. 688-698 after the unnumbered opening PB for p. 687; five folio milestones mark ff. 285r, 285v, 286r, 286v, and 287r.

formStatements.description: A 312-line poem in thirty-nine eight-line stanzas. The prose-like Latin rubric and brief quotations do not make the whole work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The poem systematically interprets physical church elements as signs of doctrine, clergy, laity, virtue, and spiritual life.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: MacCracken's modern English introduction and Longfellow quotation belong to the edition layer and are excluded from the work's primary language.

scope.description: Manuscript rubric, opening commandment gloss, and repeated biblical and liturgical quotations within the Middle English poem

notes: The catalog summary uses circa 1475 from modern scholarship but preserves the official witness record's broader fifteenth-century date.
MacCracken's unfavorable aesthetic judgments and Westminster speculation are retained only as edition-level evidence, not adopted as neutral work description or secure localization.
Modern English in the article introduction and the current catalog does not make the abstract medieval poem trilingual.

next_steps: Collate the complete poem against Book I of the Rationale and other medieval church-dedication or architectural-allegory compilations.
Separate verbal translation, thematic borrowing, common exegetical tradition, and later adaptation.
Do not identify a physical Latin exemplar without textual and provenance evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin text or texts underlie Magnificencia Ecclesie, and how directly does the Middle English poem depend on Durandus's Rationale divinorum officiorum?

next_steps: Inspect the faint attribution annotation on f. 285r and compare it palaeographically with authenticated Stow annotations across the complete manuscript.
Consult current work on the relevant R.3.21 booklet, its scribe, paper stock, dialect, and circulation.
Reassess MacCracken's rhyme argument and distinguish London booklet production from work composition.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous translator's date and place be narrowed independently of the surviving witness, and what evidence exactly generated the Lydgate attribution?

next_steps: Check the current DIMEV/NIMEV record and recent work-specific bibliography by incipit and rubric.
Search manuscript catalogs for the complete incipit, explicit, and Latin rubric rather than the conventional title alone.
Retain 'unique witness' as a supported discovery tag based on the consulted register, not as proof that no other copy exists.
openQuestions.question: Does Trinity R.3.21 remain the only known witness, or have post-1920 manuscript discoveries or reidentifications expanded the census?

assertion.notes: Middle English language and the poem's English ecclesiastical setting support the broad cultural context. MacCracken's speculation about Westminster is not promoted to a composition-place claim.
No named medieval author is secure. The catalog heading and Stow's list preserve Lydgate attribution history without establishing authorship.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: The manuscript rubric explicitly calls the tract translated into English, but the exact Latin source and degree of adaptation remain unresolved.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: MacCracken reports that John Stow attributed the poem to Lydgate and rejects the claim on rhyme and style; modern scholarship calls the poem anonymous.

notes: MacCracken also authored the article's modern introduction and supplied two editorial footnotes; those prose contributions are not medieval work authorship.

scope.description: Immediate 1909 printed edition of the medieval poem

subjects.label: Church architecture and its spiritual symbolism
The church as building, institution, and Christian community
Sacraments, commandments, and reverence for the church
Clergy, laity, preaching, and ecclesiastical order
Pride, lechery, covetousness, charity, and spiritual building

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse tract asks why the church is magnified and reads its material fabric as a spiritual and social body. Walls, stones, towers, steeple, weathercock, cross, aisles, windows, pillars, seats, steps, and altar signify the church militant and triumphant, Scripture, clergy, laity, contemplation, charity, and Christ. The poem opens with the first three commandments, urges reverence and disciplined conduct in church, and closes by excluding pride, lechery, and covetousness from the living edifice of Christian people.

The XML contains 329 L elements, but only 312 belong to the medieval poem; the other seventeen form MacCracken's quotation from Longfellow in the modern introduction. The poem itself consists of thirty-nine LG elements, each containing eight direct L children.

assertion.notes: A conventional Latin title taken from the manuscript rubric, not evidence of named medieval authorship.

titles.supplied_by: Henry Noble MacCracken's 1909 edition and subsequent scholarship

assertion.notes: The rubric describes a short tract translated concisely into English on the magnificence of the church.

titles.value: Hic sequitur parvus tractatus compendiose in Anglicis translatus de magnificencia ecclesie

assertion.notes: Retained as catalog attribution history only, distinct from the manuscript's visible Latin rubric. MacCracken rejects Lydgate's authorship, and modern scholarship treats the poem as anonymous.

titles.value: And I wyll pray whyle ye rede eftsone in lyke wyse

Walls, stones, towers, steeple, weathercock, cross, aisles, windows, pillars, seats, steps, and altar signify the church militant and triumphant, Scripture, clergy, laity, contemplation, charity, and Christ. The poem opens with the first three commandments, urges reverence and disciplined conduct in church, and closes by excluding pride, lechery, and covetousness from the living edifice of Christian people.
